what: Unify M<=32 config: remove K>1024 special case, use BSM=8/BSN=128/BSK=256/NW=4/NS=2/waves=2/.cg for ALL M<=32 shapes regardless of K. v189/v191 tried this but LB score was unparseable both times — never properly evaluated. For M=16 K>1024 shapes, legacy BSM=32 wastes 50% of rows (16 useful + 16 padding), vs BSM=8 with 2 fully-utilized blocks.
why: The M<=32 K>1024 config (BSM=32/BSN=64/BSK=512/NW=8/NS=1) has BSM=32 which wastes computation for M=16 (50% padding). The K<=1024 config (BSM=8/BSN=128/BSK=256/NW=4/NS=2/.cg) uses BSM=8 with software pipelining. v189 and v191 both attempted this change but the popcorn LB score parser failed both times, so the optimization was never measured. Third attempt with current tooling.
override: v189/v191 had unparseable LB scores, optimization was never properly evaluated

what: Eliminate B_w/B_sc data_ptr caching — always compute views inline. On LB (recheck=True), data_ptr changes every call so cache always misses. Removing the dict lookups (buf.get, buf[_b_ptr]) and conditional saves ~200ns per call across all fused paths.
why: LB creates new tensors every iteration via generate_input, so data_ptr is always different. The cache check (B_shuffle.data_ptr(), dict comparison, conditional branch) is pure overhead on LB. Simply computing B_w = B_shuffle.view(uint8).reshape(...) inline each time avoids this. View operations are O(1) metadata changes.

what_failed: v209: Unified M<=32 config (remove K>1024 branch) was neutral since all LB M=32 shapes use K=512. v210: Inline B view computation (eliminate data_ptr caching) was +1.9% worse — Python tensor view/reshape per-call overhead outweighs saved dict comparison. After 240+ proposals, kernel is at optimization floor. The 9% gap to #1 (8.23us vs 8.98us) requires a fundamentally different algorithmic approach not found in aiter library or AMD kernel ecosystem.
